Leapfrog Remedies
Growth Strategy & Handover

SOSTAC Framework · Phase 1

Prepared byNeil Minty, Personaify Prepared forStephanie Drax, Leapfrog Remedies DateJune 2026 Versionv29 — Working Draft StatusIn progress. Sections expand as remaining data and market research are added.
Executive Summary

Five Imperatives · The Commercial Picture · The Revenue Gap

The Commercial Picture

Leapfrog is a stronger business than the surface data suggests. New customers are up 74% year-on-year. New subscribers are up 303%. MRR is £53,946 (61.3% of total revenue). The science behind the products is genuinely differentiated. Press coverage is earned, not bought. The brand has arrived at the right moment in a category that is about to matter.

The problems are structural, not existential. Subscriber cohort M1 — the rate at which new subscribers place a second subscription order the following month — has compressed from 52.6% (Jun 2025) to 22.1% (Apr 2026). MoM all-buyer retention has held above 30% throughout, sustained by an increasingly loyal existing subscriber base: 77% of April buyers were returning customers. The dependency on that base is itself a risk. 6,104 customers bought once and never returned. The paid media model has been optimising for ROAS — the wrong metric for a subscription business. The brand is absent from every AI search query in its category. The team is configured for a smaller business than this plan requires.

The 2026 revenue plan is £1,672,710. Growth Room modelling puts the range at £1.49m (conservative) to £1.99m (optimistic). The plan sits within that range. The £502k spread between the two scenarios is determined by execution of the six imperatives below.

The Six Imperatives

1
Establish UK lactoferrin category authority

The colostrum category is in demonstrable growth in the US (203% YoY, SPINS retail data). No dominant UK lactoferrin brand exists. Leapfrog is cited in 0 of 5 tracked AI query clusters. GEO fixes are the infrastructure prerequisite — but the founder programme, science estate, press strategy, and Selfridges positioning must activate in parallel to establish genuine category authority.

2
Fix subscription retention

Subscriber cohort M1 — new subscribers placing a second subscription order the following month — has fallen to 22.1%. MoM all-buyer retention has held above 30%, but 77% of April buyers were returning customers. The business is increasingly dependent on an existing subscriber base to sustain that figure. The retention architecture is a seven-flow system covering onboarding, churn prevention, win-back, pre-cancel save, and the December 2025 cohort re-engagement. Order 4 is the structural threshold — across IMMUNE and SNOOZE, customers who reach their fourth order show materially higher long-term retention. Every flow is designed to get subscribers there.

3
Reframe paid media around subscriber CAC

ROAS is the wrong KPI. The new paid media agency must be briefed on variable CAC ceilings by product before any budget is committed. IMMUNE (£856 avg LTV) can support a materially higher acquisition cost than SNOOZE (£611 avg LTV). Build the model first.

4
Build a defensible pricing architecture

Every subscriber pays approximately 20% below one-time retail as the standard subscription rate. The open questions: what is the net margin on a subscription first order at scale, is the 20% saving the right depth, and is it framed as a reason to subscribe or just a default? SNOOZE requires specific diagnosis before any intervention.

5
Diagnose and rebuild SNOOZE

SNOOZE has lost 20 percentage points of new customer acquisition share in twelve months. The 20% low-rating rate (vs 2.8% for IMMUNE) and the declining subscriber base make this a commercial priority, not a background concern. The diagnosis — positioning problem or pricing problem — must be resolved before any intervention. The WTP survey, the review data, and the clearance strategy are the three immediate workstreams.

6
Establish FOCUS as a standalone commercial product

FOCUS has 589 orders, 423 customers, and £58,828 revenue across 13 months — but 88.4% of buyers also purchased IMMUNE or SNOOZE. It is functioning as a cross-sell, not an independent product. The positioning, subscription conversion architecture, and entry product decision are the three workstreams required to change that.

S · Situation

Where are we now?

Commercial baseline, customer data, traffic, GEO visibility, and what the data is telling us.

Leapfrog is operating in a supplement category that is early-stage in the UK but already in demonstrable growth in the US. The brand has real commercial momentum — new customers up 74%, new subscribers up 303%, MRR at £53,946 — but structural problems that will compound if not addressed.

Market Context: The UK Immune Supplement Category & the Lactoferrin Opportunity

Source: Market sizing from Grand View Research, IMARC Group, Straits Research (2024–2026). Cultural and retail trend data from SPINS and Nutritional Outlook. All market sizing figures are directional.

The UK immune supplement category

  • UK dietary supplements market: estimated at USD 4.79 billion in 2024, projected to reach USD 9.65 billion by 2033 at 8.1% CAGR. Immune health accounts for 12.6% of UK supplement revenue.
  • UK immune health supplements: estimated at USD 1.2 billion in 2023, growing to USD 2.78 billion by 2030 at 12.7% CAGR.
  • Natural and herbal supplements is the fastest-growing UK supplement segment — directly relevant to Leapfrog's positioning.
  • The UK lactoferrin market is described as 'early development phase' across multiple sources. Leapfrog is operating before category consolidation, not after it.

The lactoferrin opportunity

Lactoferrin is a bioactive protein with 20+ published RCTs supporting immune health benefits. It is the primary active in Leapfrog's core products. The global lactoferrin supplements market is estimated at USD 361 million in 2024 growing to USD 475 million by 2034. No dominant UK lactoferrin brand exists. Leapfrog is operating in the window before that position is defined.

Lactoferrin's position as the mechanism within the broader colostrum trend is strategically superior to positioning as a colostrum brand. Colostrum is the category. Lactoferrin is the science. Leapfrog has the science.

The colostrum trend as a demand catalyst

The colostrum category is in demonstrable growth in the US: 203% YoY in the natural channel immune health segment (SPINS, November 2025). Vital Proteins — which built the collagen category and was acquired by Nestlé — entered colostrum in January 2025, signalling mainstream category confidence. UK consumer trends have historically followed US ones. The window to establish lactoferrin category authority in the UK is open now.

The GEO audit finding — 0 of 5 tracked AI query clusters, 32 competitor domains cited instead of Leapfrog — means none of the lactoferrin or colostrum consumer search demand that is building is currently reaching Leapfrog. Structural fixes must precede the category moment, not follow it.

Brand Overview

BrandLeapfrog Remedies
FounderStephanie Drax
CategoryNatural health supplements / wellness
Core SKUsIMMUNE, FOCUS, SNOOZE, DAILY + The Trio and The Duo bundles
Key ingredientsLactoferrin (IMMUNE / FOCUS / DAILY), Lactium (SNOOZE / FOCUS)
PositioningScience-backed, founder-led, premium natural remedies
Press coverageCountry & Town House, Vanity Fair, Daily Mail, Sheerluxe, Evening Standard. Earned, not paid.
RetailSelfridges listing
Social proof4.6/5 across 903 reviews (Loox). The review volume is a commercial asset — it signals product efficacy at scale and should be structured for GEO attribution and surfaced above the fold on primary PDPs.

The press coverage and review base are two underutilised commercial assets. The coverage currently appears as a logo bar on the site with no dedicated press section. The 903 reviews at 4.6/5 are not surfaced prominently on the primary PDPs. Both need to do more work in converting visitors who arrive at the premium price threshold with no prior brand awareness.

Customer Personas

Source: Personaify review analysis. Three primary segments from Loox review data. Data transparency: 10–14 data points per persona; coverage 0.6–0.8% of total dataset. Segments reflect behavioural signals from reviews, not demographic data.

01 · The High-Stakes Health Investors

14 data points · 505 theme mentions · 0.8% coverage · 45% positive / 30% mixed / 24% negative

Uses the product for critical health needs: staying well for work, managing underlying conditions, improving sleep. Convinced of efficacy, describing results in near-magical terms. The persistent tension: strong belief in the product's value constantly challenged by its high price. Emotional tone is Conflicted Appreciation — grateful for results but frustrated by cost. Prevented from being unconditional advocates by price friction.

Key insight: The premium price is the primary barrier to advocacy — not efficacy doubt. The fix is making the value case undeniable before purchase, not reducing the price.

"I haven't had a cold in the 2 years I've been taking them. Expensive... but effective." · "It's like a little miracle tablet."

02 · The Proactive Health Planners

13 data points · 511 theme mentions · 0.7% coverage · 72% positive / 16% mixed / 12% negative

A small but highly loyal segment. Views health as a planned activity, not a reaction. Uses supplements as a preventative shield for the whole family — preparing for travel, school return, or early-symptom response. Confident, trusting, empowered. The highest positive sentiment of the three segments.

Key insight: This segment treats the product as a non-negotiable household tool. The family use case is a messaging angle largely absent from current site content. It also signals multi-pack and bundle purchase intent.

"Great! Am sure they help the whole family. Always take with us when we are travelling too."

03 · The First-Sign Believers

10 data points · 1,247 theme mentions · 0.6% coverage · 51% positive / 28% mixed / 21% negative

Vigilant health guardians who use the product as their first line of defence. Highly attuned to their bodies — they act at the first sign of a cold. Primarily positive and advocating, with a significant undercurrent of mixed feelings related to practical concerns — running out, pack size, inconsistent secondary benefits.

Key insight: The first-sign use case implies a reorder pattern built around illness onset, not calendar frequency. The subscription model may not be the right frame — a rapid-response pack format with a different cadence could serve them better.

"Love this product. First sign of a cold my go-to is Immune. I give it to my whole family."

Cross-persona pricing observation: price sensitivity appears in all three segments to varying degrees. The High-Stakes Investors show the most friction. The Proactive Planners and First-Sign Believers are less price-driven when the product is proving its value. This supports a messaging-first approach to pricing rather than a price reduction.

Revenue Forecast: Condensed P&L

Growth Room modelling shows a 2026 range of £1.490m (conservative) to £1.992m (optimistic). The plan of £1.673m sits within that range. The £502k spread between scenarios is determined by the execution of the levers set out in this document.

20252026202720282029
Revenue£989,818£1,672,710£3,254,577£5,332,268£10,673,971
DTC£893,726£1,587,315£2,894,577£4,732,268£9,653,971
B2B / Wholesale£96,093£85,395£360,000£600,000£1,020,000
Gross Profit£720,088£1,162,099£2,231,122£3,648,187£7,351,742
GP %73%69%69%68%69%
Contribution£318,839£484,007£1,191,192£1,850,139£4,209,042
Contribution %32%29%37%35%39%
MER2.47x2.47x3.13x2.97x3.40x
Operating Profit£151,976£202,016£705,213£964,215£3,017,585
Op Profit %15%12%22%18%28%
Net Profit£155,022£204,820£708,017£967,019£3,020,389
Net Profit %16%12%22%18%28%
  • Revenue ambition is aggressive: 3.2x growth 2025→2027, 10.7x by 2029. DTC carries 90%+ throughout.
  • Gross margin holding at 68–73% is the structural advantage. Must be protected as volume grows.
  • Contribution margin dips to 29% in 2026, the paid media scaling year. MER must move from 2.47x to 3.13x by 2027.
  • B2B/Wholesale forecast grows from £96k to £1m by 2029, currently anchored on one Selfridges listing.

■ Raw ingredient costs are variable with no hedging. High WOC on some SKUs creates material cash exposure. Requires commercial review.

■ SNOOZE has lost 20 percentage points of new customer acquisition share: from 1-in-3 new customers in 2025 to roughly 1-in-9 in 2026. Losing product diversity in new customer acquisition is a structural risk regardless of which product it favours.

The GA4 / Shopify Reconciliation

GA4 shows total revenue YTD down 43% (£180k vs £317k). Shopify shows new customer revenue up 56% (£254k vs £163k). These are not contradictory — they are measuring different things.

  • GA4 revenue attribution is session-based. Subscription recurring orders are typically attributed to Direct or Unassigned in GA4 because the checkout journey does not originate from a tracked session. As subscription revenue has grown (MRR now £53,946), more revenue appears as Direct or Unassigned in GA4.
  • The 59% decline in Direct revenue in GA4 is partly a reporting artefact of this attribution gap.
  • Paid Social shows 10,808 sessions but £2,274 in GA4 revenue. The Shopify data shows 790 new subscribers acquired in 2026 at subscription first-order revenue of £46,006. Most of this will not be attributed to Paid Social in GA4 if the subscription checkout journey breaks the session chain.

The headline finding: the ROAS model is measuring the wrong thing. Paid Social looks like it is generating volume without revenue. In reality it is generating subscribers whose LTV materialises over 14 orders, none of which are visible in a session-based ROAS calculation.

■ ROAS optimisation actively works against this model. A ROAS-optimised campaign prefers a one-time buyer at £68 who converts immediately over a subscriber at £61 who generates £856 over 14 orders. The paid media brief to any new resource must be built on new subscriber CAC, not ROAS.

Traffic: GA4 Channel Overview

Source: GA4 Sessions by Channel Group. Interpret revenue figures as directional only — subscription attribution gaps make channel revenue materially understated.

ChannelSessions 2026Sessions 2025% ChangeGA4 Rev 2026GA4 Rev 2025
Direct38,78442,649−9.1%£101,069£249,362

  • Organic Social is the one channel performing as expected: sessions up 38%, revenue up 26%. Deserves more resource.
  • Cross-network down 71% in sessions. Something has changed in this channel configuration. Revenue per session remains high — investigate what has been turned off or rerouted.
  • Organic Search down 14%, consistent with the GEO/SEO findings. The site is not winning new search visibility.

Subscription Health

MonthNew SubscribersRecurring OrdersSubscription Revenue
Dec 2025672792£86,077

Feb 2026201838£58,100
Mar 2026219953£69,317
Apr 2026196818£56,912
May 2026131463£35,879
New subscriber acquisition is Q1-weighted — December and January reflect the seasonal peak. The step-down from January to May is expected and consistent with prior year patterns. What matters is what the cohort data shows about the subscribers acquired during that peak: the December 2025 cohort (652 subscribers, the largest ever acquired) is retaining at 9.8% by M5. The acquisition numbers are not the problem. What happens after acquisition is.

Subscriber Cohort Retention

M1 = month after first subscription order. Green ≥70% · Amber 50–69% · Orange 30–49% · Red <30%.

CohortSizeM1M2M3M4M5M6
Jun 20253852.6%50.0%47.4%42.1%42.1%42.1%
Jul 20255942.4%37.3%47.5%37.3%33.9%35.6%
Aug 20258643.0%53.5%43.0%41.9%29.1%29.1%
Sep 202522537.3%49.8%44.0%41.3%27.1%37.8%
Oct 202531642.4%50.6%40.5%30.7%24.1%28.2%
Nov 202528850.7%49.0%39.6%34.4%23.3%12.8%
Dec 202565240.6%36.7%32.1%23.8%9.8%—
Jan 202638640.7%47.7%36.8%17.6%——
Feb 202620142.8%42.3%28.4%———
Mar 202621335.7%28.6%————
Apr 202619522.1%—————
May 2026131——————

Subscriber cohort M1 — the proportion of new subscribers who placed a second subscription order the following month — declined from 52.6% (Jun 2025) to 22.1% (Apr 2026). This is distinct from MoM all-buyer retention, which held above 30% throughout. The Dec 2025 cohort (652 subscribers, the largest) is showing accelerated decay at 9.8% by M5. The Jun–Jul 2025 cohorts are stabilising at 35–42% by M6 — the sustainable floor to aim for.

Subscriber cohort M1 at 22.1% in Apr 2026 is the primary retention risk. If this is confirmed at M2 and M3, intervention is required immediately.
March 2026 cohort (158 subscribers) shows 51.3% M1 rate — a 14 to 20 percentage point drop from the September to November 2025 band. This is the sharpest single-month retention deterioration in the dataset. Was there a change in the Appstle subscription configuration, onboarding flow, or acquisition channel mix in March 2026?

First Product Purchased: Retention and LTV

How first product predicts long-term retention and lifetime value. Subscription customers only.

ProductCustomersAvg LTV30d Ret.90d Ret.Full-Price Acq.Avg Orders
IMMUNE1,016£85692.6%88.0%47.9%14.02
SNOOZE467£61183.7%74.5%57.0%8.48
FOCUS41£29494.7%75.0%73.2%4.22
THE TRIO †148£178*61.8%45.2%68.2% acquisition1.18
THE DUO †56£117*——48.2% acquisition1.13
DAILY5£75——100%1.20

* Bundle LTV figures capture bundle-product spend only and do not include downstream single-SKU orders. TRIO-acquired customers who subsequently buy IMMUNE will carry IMMUNE-level LTV on top of this figure. † THE TRIO: 11 months of data (Jul 2025–Jun 2026), 148 customers — treat as directional. THE DUO: 5 months of data (Jan–Jun 2026), 56 customers — too early for conclusions.

  • IMMUNE is the highest-value entry point: £856 avg LTV, 88% 90-day retention, 14 avg orders. Protect the IMMUNE price point.
  • SNOOZE shows lower 90-day retention than IMMUNE (74.5% vs 88%) and has lost significant acquisition share. Two hypotheses: value communication problem or pricing problem. Both should be investigated before any intervention.
  • FOCUS shows strong retention (94.7% at 30d) but 41 customers is not a sufficient base for firm conclusions.
  • THE TRIO is an acquisition vehicle: 68% of its 148 customers had never bought from Leapfrog before. Of those who reorder, IMMUNE is the dominant next product (23 of 36 follow-on buyers). Cohort is too small and too recent for strategic conclusions — run bundle data in Growth Room as it matures.
  • THE DUO: 56 customers, 5 months of data. Too early to interpret.
  • DAILY: only 5 subscribers. The strategic case for DAILY rests on the investment already committed, not current performance.
30% of first purchases are subscriptions, 70% transactional. The one-time to subscription gap is £181 per customer (£57 LTV vs £238). Converting first-time buyers to subscribers is the highest near-term compounding lever.

Transactional Customer Base: Repeat Purchase Gap

Source: Growth Room customer_orders — most recent pull. Updated figures reflect confirmed one-time buyers only with consistent tagging.

MetricValue
One-time buyer avg LTV£57 (1 order)
Repeat buyer avg LTV£238 (3.3 orders)
Incremental LTV gap£181 per customer converted
Confirmed one-time buyer base6,104 customers
Median time to 2nd order45 days among those who do return
Win-back window~60 days, after which re-engagement drops sharply without intervention
Conservative recovery (5%)~305 customers · ~£55,300 incremental LTV (IMMUNE basis)
Optimistic recovery (10%)~610 customers · ~£110,500 incremental LTV (blended basis)
Current win-back flows trigger at 90 days or beyond — well outside the re-engagement window. The practical fix is a post-purchase sequence targeting days 20–45. The full seven-flow retention architecture is detailed in the Tactics section.

Subscription Acquisition: Discount Depth and Pricing Shift

Source: Growth Room customer_orders, 30,116 orders, 2,966 unique subscribers, Jun 2024 to May 2026.

An important labelling point: 'full price' in this analysis means the standard subscription price with no additional promotional discount applied. Every subscriber pays approximately 20% below one-time retail by default — that is the subscription price point, not a discount code.

Phase 1 (Jun 2024 – Aug 2025): additional promotional discounting

  • Between 27% and 72% of new subscribers in this period were arriving with an additional discount on top of the standard subscription price — likely a 10% promotional code at checkout.
  • Average additional discount depth 1–8%, trending higher through 2025 Q1 and Q2.
  • The 10–19% additional discount cluster accounts for 411 orders (13.9% of all first subscription orders across the full period).

Phase 2 (Sep 2025 onwards): standard subscription pricing only

  • The additional promotional discount layer was removed in September 2025. Cohort sizes jumped simultaneously: 184, 259, 246, 557, 294, 138, 158 per month. The brand scaled after removing the additional promotional layer.
  • The September 2025 transition is sharp and unambiguous in the order data.
