GEO / AEO Baseline

Source: Growth Room GEO Agent audit (14 May 2026) + HubSpot AEO Grader (31 March 2026).

Leapfrog is currently invisible in AI search. The fixes are structural, not creative. The science estate exists — it is simply not formatted for machine extraction.

MetricValue
Site-wide GEO score51 / 100 (Products 53 · Science 49 · Journal 51)
Citation share0 of 5 tracked query clusters. Leapfrog is absent from every tracked AI answer.
Competitor domains cited32 domains cited instead across 5 clusters
ChatGPT AEO score (Mar 26)31 / 100
Perplexity AEO score (Mar 26)57 / 100
Gemini AEO score (Mar 26)45 / 100
Brand recognition (AI)8–15 / 100 across all platforms
ProductGEO ScoreParaphrase RiskRewrite PriorityCritical Gap
DAILY40High72 (most urgent)No numeric claims, no science language
IMMUNE48High66Claim specificity weak despite entity clarity
SNOOZE62High57Lactium present but no measurable claims
FOCUS63High56Entity clarity 100. Claim Specificity 20.
  • Claim Specificity scores 20/100 across all product pages. The science estate (20+ RCTs for lactoferrin, 6 for Lactium) exists but is not structured for AI retrieval. The fixes are structural, not creative.
  • llms.txt store description is auto-generated promotional copy ('SHOP NOW'). Must be replaced immediately.
  • No schema on any page. Science estate is invisible to machine extraction until schema is in place.
  • Immunity cluster: absent. Sleep cluster: absent. Focus & Energy: absent. General Wellness: absent.
  • There is no dedicated press section on the site. The Vogue and Vanity Fair coverage appears as logos only. A structured press/editorial page should be live before any active press outreach begins.

O · Objectives

What does success look like? Targets tied to the P&L; forecast and grounded in the data.

Commercial Objectives

The P&L projects £10.7m revenue by 2029. The path from £990k (2025) to that number requires specific conditions to hold in each year.

YearRevenue targetPrimary conditions requiredPrimary risk if not met
2026£1,672,710 plan
£1,490,000–£1,992,000 range		M1 retention stabilises at 35%+. New paid media agency briefed on subscriber CAC before spend committed. SNOOZE diagnosis complete.£502k gap closes toward conservative if retention stays at 22.1% and paid media continues on ROAS.
2027£3,254,577 (+95% on 2026)Contribution margin recovers to 37% from 29%. MER improves to 3.13x. B2B/wholesale established at £360k. Subscription mix maintained as acquisition scales.If MER does not improve from 2.47x, the paid media scaling year destroys margin. B2B/wholesale at £360k requires a channel strategy that does not yet exist.
2028£5,332,268 (+64% on 2027)DTC subscription base compounds on improved retention. B2B/wholesale scales to £600k. Team infrastructure supports growth without founder bottleneck.IMMUNE concentration (84.2% of new customers) becomes a structural risk if a competing product enters the UK lactoferrin category.
2029£10,673,971 (+100% on 2028)Category authority established. Lactoferrin recognised as a mainstream UK supplement category. Leapfrog cited as the primary brand. B2B/wholesale at £1m.A better-resourced competitor establishing category authority first makes this number unreachable through DTC alone.
2026 Specifics
2026 revenue plan£1,672,710
Growth Room conservative£1,490,000 (anchored on current retention and moderated acquisition)
Growth Room optimistic£1,992,000 (requires trajectory to hold and retention improvements to land)
The lever£502k spread between conservative and optimistic scenarios. The plan sits within this range. This document defines how to close the gap to optimistic.
Gross margin floor68% minimum — must be protected through any pricing changes
Contribution margin 2026→202729% (2026 scaling year) recovering to 37% (2027)
MER trajectory2.47x (current) → 3.0x+ (end 2026) → 3.13x (2027)
B2B / Wholesale 2027£360,000 — groundwork must be laid in 2026

Retention Objectives

  • Stabilise M1 subscription retention — target 40%+ M1 within 3 months. Feasibility depends on isolating the cause of decline via Klaviyo audit and Appstle config review.
  • Reduce 90-day churn from 37.2% — target below 30% within 6 months.
  • Win-back sequence: deploy days 20–45 trigger. 5% conversion of the forward transactional flow adds approximately £44k incremental LTV in 2026 at current acquisition rates.
  • December 2025 cohort re-engagement: 652 subscribers, 9.8% M5 retention. Segment into active (do not contact), paused, and cancelled before any outreach is sent.

Acquisition Objectives

  • Reframe paid media KPI: switch from ROAS to new subscriber CAC by product before any new paid media spend is committed.
  • Build variable CAC ceiling model: IMMUNE, SNOOZE, FOCUS — derived from LTV, gross margin, and target payback period.
  • Improve blended MER from 2.47x to 3.0x+ by end of 2026 through better cohort targeting, not necessarily more spend.
  • Diagnose SNOOZE acquisition decline before setting any targets or increasing spend on SNOOZE.
  • PR and influencer resource: in place before the Marvellous Insights SEO programme and science estate work are complete.

Pricing Objectives

  • Deploy WTP survey to two Klaviyo segments: current SNOOZE subscribers and churned SNOOZE customers. Ensure product selector anchors Van Westendorp questions at SKU level before deployment.
  • Establish actual net margin on subscription first order at the 20% below one-time rate.
  • Model SKU-level pricing architecture: should IMMUNE, SNOOZE, FOCUS, and DAILY carry differentiated price points?
  • Reframe subscription proposition — test 'Never run out' framing against '20% off' with a defined success metric.

GEO / AI Visibility Objectives

  • Move ChatGPT AEO score from 31/100 to 55+ within 6 months of structural fixes being implemented.
  • Achieve citation in 3 of 5 tracked AI query clusters (currently 0 of 5) within 6 months.
  • Establish Leapfrog as the cited source for lactoferrin and Lactium benefit claims in AI search.
  • Build and publish the dedicated press/editorial page before any active press outreach begins.

FOCUS Objectives

  • Increase FOCUS subscription rate from 24.8% to 40%+ within 6 months through post-purchase sequence and FOC-090 subscription upsell.
  • Resolve FOC-015 strategy: Option A (retire as standalone, use as seeding tool) or Option B (redesign as explicit trial SKU).
  • Build standalone FOCUS customer base: 49 FOCUS-only customers in 13 months is the current baseline. Target 100+ within 6 months.
  • Establish FOCUS sequence attribution: determine where in the IMMUNE customer journey FOCUS is being added, to define optimal cross-sell trigger timing.

DAILY Objectives

  • Define commercial KPIs for the live campaign before paid amplification spend is committed.
  • Set a provisional CAC ceiling based on current LTV data (£75 at 1.2 orders).
  • Establish a 30-day retention gate from the creator cohort before activating paid amplification.
  • Acquire 100+ DAILY subscribers with clean cohort isolation to establish a retention baseline.
  • Define what 90-day performance looks like in subscriber and retention terms before any further investment decision.

S · Strategy

Six strategic imperatives govern all decisions: (1) Establish UK lactoferrin category authority. (2) Increase subscription mix. (3) Reframe paid media around new subscriber CAC. (4) Build a defensible pricing architecture. (5) Diagnose and rebuild SNOOZE. (6) Establish FOCUS as a standalone commercial product. Everything else is a tactic in service of these six.

Strategic Imperative 1 · Establish Category Authority Before the Moment Arrives

Leapfrog's claim is not simply that it sells lactoferrin. It is that it was there first, has the clinical evidence, and has the press coverage to prove it. That is a chronological and evidential authority claim that no UK competitor can currently replicate.

The press coverage — Country & Town House, Vanity Fair, Daily Mail, Sheerluxe, Evening Standard — is currently a logo bar on the site with no dedicated press section. A structured editorial page with pull quotes and article summaries signals authority to new visitors at the premium price threshold, gives journalists a reference point, and creates citable third-party content that feeds GEO. Half-day build. No new content required. Should be live before any active press outreach begins.

Five vehicles for establishing category authority:

VehicleDescription
1. Founder authority programmeStephanie positioned as the person who brought clinical lactoferrin to the UK consumer market. Podcast appearances, press briefings timed to the category moment, LinkedIn long-form on the science. The full press coverage set is the credential. Owner: PR resource.
2. Science publishing programmeThe 20+ lactoferrin RCTs and 6 Lactium RCTs are a content moat no competitor can quickly replicate. A lactoferrin science hub — lay summaries, referenced claims, FAQ format — feeds GEO, builds consumer trust, and makes Leapfrog the primary reference for journalists and influencers entering the category. Owner: Jon + Stephanie + PR resource.
3. Category intercept content'What is lactoferrin?', 'Lactoferrin vs colostrum' — the pages a category authority owns before the category arrives. Currently 32 competitor domains are cited in AI search on these queries instead of Leapfrog. Owner: Jon + PR resource.
4. Trade and press strategyThe SPINS 203% YoY data, the Vital Proteins colostrum entry, and the UK market timing constitute a news story. A proactive press programme with a defined media list turns the market context into active coverage. Owner: PR resource.
5. Selfridges as authority signalThe Selfridges listing is currently underdeveloped in brand communications. It should be doing more work in press materials, on the website, and in influencer briefings. It answers the credibility question before it is asked.
The timing argument: the UK lactoferrin category positioning window is open now. In 12–18 months, better-resourced brands will arrive. Category authority cannot be bought at that point — it has to be established before the category is crowded.

Strategic Imperative 2 · Increase Subscription Mix

MRR is currently £53,946 (61.3% of total revenue). New subscribers are up 303% YoY. The growth is real. The problem is what happens after acquisition: subscriber cohort M1 has declined from 52.6% (Jun 2025) to 22.1% (Apr 2026) — meaning the brand is acquiring more subscribers than ever while losing them faster than ever. MoM all-buyer retention has held above 30%, sustained by returning customers who now make up 77% of monthly buyers. That dependency is the structural risk.

The Order 4 threshold

Analysis of IMMUNE and SNOOZE retention curves shows a consistent pattern: subscribers who reach their fourth order show materially higher long-term retention than those who churn before it. Order 4 is the structural threshold. The entire retention architecture — seven flows, covering onboarding through win-back — is designed to get subscribers to order 4. Every intervention is evaluated against this objective.

Problem A — Subscriber cohort M1 compression

The subscriber commits at purchase but does not renew. This is a post-purchase experience problem, not an acquisition problem. Subscriber cohort M1 — new subscribers placing a second order the following month — has fallen from 52.6% to 22.1%. The Dec 2025 cohort (672 subscribers, the largest ever acquired) is showing 9.8% retention at M5.

Problem B — SNOOZE subscriber base thinning

SNOOZE has lost 20 percentage points of new customer acquisition share (31.5% → 11.5%). The subscriber base is becoming concentrated in IMMUNE. Concentration in one SKU is a structural risk.

  • Make subscription the default purchase journey on all PDPs. One-time purchase becomes a conscious downgrade, not the path of least resistance.
  • Fix post-purchase onboarding — the first 30 days determine whether a subscriber renews.
  • Segment by product. IMMUNE subscribers retain at 88% at 90 days. SNOOZE at 74.5%. Do not apply a single onboarding flow to both.
  • Set a variable CAC ceiling by product. IMMUNE's LTV (£856, 14 orders) supports a substantially higher acquisition cost than SNOOZE (£611, 8.5 orders).

Strategic Imperative 3 · Reframe Paid Media Around New Subscriber CAC

Social Nucleus have been managing to ROAS as the primary KPI. ROAS optimises for revenue per pound spent in the attribution window. A subscription first order at £61 (discounted 20%) looks worse than a one-time purchase at £68 in a ROAS model. The campaign algorithm will always prefer the one-time buyer.

ROAS as the primary KPI is structurally incompatible with a subscription-led growth model. It optimises for the wrong cohort and systematically undervalues the most valuable customer the brand can acquire.

The variable CAC model:

Product90d RetentionAvg LTVAvg OrdersImplied CAC Ceiling
IMMUNE88.0%£85614.02To be modelled — materially higher than SNOOZE
SNOOZE74.5%£6118.48To be modelled — baseline ceiling
FOCUS75.0%£2944.22Insufficient data — monitor
DAILY—£751.20Insufficient data — build retention baseline first
  • The IMMUNE CAC ceiling will be materially higher than the SNOOZE ceiling given the 13-point retention differential and £245 LTV gap.
  • CAC ceilings should be modelled against full-price acquisition economics (Sep 2025 onwards), not blended historical figures.
  • The SNOOZE acquisition strategy needs to run in parallel — different creative, different audience, different retention expectation.

Strategic Imperative 4 · Build a Defensible Pricing Architecture

The brand stopped applying an additional promotional discount in September 2025 — and acquisition volume grew simultaneously. Every subscriber still pays approximately 20% below one-time retail by default — that is the subscription price point, not a promotional code.

  • SKU-level pricing model: should IMMUNE, SNOOZE, FOCUS, and DAILY carry different price points based on their LTV economics?
  • Confirm subscription margin model: verify gross margin on subscription first order across all SKUs.
  • Reframe the subscription proposition: 'Never run out' is a more durable reason to subscribe than '20% off'.
  • Deploy WTP survey before any pricing decisions are made.

SNOOZE: two competing hypotheses — positioning (benefit not communicating value at price) or pricing (sleep category is more competitive). The resolution requires qualitative research with churned SNOOZE subscribers before any intervention.

Strategic Imperative 5 · Diagnose and Rebuild SNOOZE

SNOOZE is the most commercially exposed product in the range. First product acquisition share has fallen from 31.5% to 11.5% in twelve months. The 20% low-rating rate (vs 2.8% for IMMUNE) is a meaningful signal at n=237. The subscriber base is thinning. The overstock position creates a parallel cash exposure problem. These are not separate issues — they all require a diagnosis before any intervention is made.

The diagnosis question is binary: positioning problem or pricing problem. The WTP survey answers it. The distinction matters because the two interventions are different in cost, speed, and margin impact. Fixing positioning costs messaging effort and content time. Fixing pricing costs margin and requires subscriber volume recovery to compensate.

Three immediate workstreams

  • Deploy WTP survey to current SNOOZE subscribers and churned SNOOZE customers. The churned cohort is the most diagnostically valuable. Results determine the intervention path.
  • Resolve clearance strategy for slow-moving SNOOZE inventory before the handover session. Options: markdown floor, B2B/wholesale placement, bundle promotion. Each has a different margin implication and each must be modelled before a decision is made.
  • Rewrite SNOOZE PDP subscription CTA structure (from P1 tactics) regardless of diagnosis outcome. Defer value communication changes until the positioning vs pricing question is resolved.

If the diagnosis returns a positioning verdict

Rewrite the Lactium science for consumer audiences. Reset expectations at point of purchase — be explicit about who SNOOZE is for and what it can and cannot guarantee. Build the Lactium vs Melatonin comparison page. Give this 90 days and measure acquisition rate against the pre-decline baseline before any pricing change is considered.

If the diagnosis returns a pricing verdict

The pricing model in Appendix C defines the options. A 10% price reduction requires 16% more subscribers to hold margin — against a base already declining 36% YoY. A 10–15% reduction is the range where the required subscriber uplift is challenging but not implausible. Do not execute before SNOOZE-specific WTP data is available.

SNOOZE overstock is a cash exposure problem that exists independently of the strategic diagnosis. The clearance strategy must be agreed before any other SNOOZE decision is made.

Strategic Imperative 6 · Establish FOCUS as a Standalone Commercial Product

FOCUS has a commercially encouraging data profile — FOC-090 at £237 AOV, repeat customers averaging 5.4 orders and £454 total spend, 30-day median reorder interval — but 88.4% of buyers came through IMMUNE or SNOOZE. The product has not been marketed as a standalone proposition. That is the problem to fix.

The positioning

FOCUS is for people who are already performing well and want to maintain the quality of their thinking — through demanding days, without stimulants, without compromise. Not deficit framing. Not for people struggling. For people protecting something they value.

The FOCUS vs SNOOZE tension — resolved

Both FOCUS and SNOOZE contain Lactoferrin and Lactium. The resolution is context and timing, not mechanism. Lactium reduces cortisol. In the morning, lower cortisol means less cognitive interference — clear thinking. In the evening, lower cortisol means easier transition to sleep. The biology is the same. The application window is different. FOCUS is a morning and daytime product. SNOOZE is an evening product.

This framing is honest, coherent, and defensible. It also opens a whole-day narrative — IMMUNE for resilience, FOCUS for the working day, SNOOZE for recovery and sleep.

Two customer groups

GroupDescriptionH2 priority
Group A — The Leapfrog loyalistAlready using IMMUNE or SNOOZE. Understands the ingredient logic. Adds FOCUS as a deliberate extension of an existing health practice. High intent, high trust. 88% of current base.Cross-sell and retention — lower acquisition cost
Group B — The intentional performerHigh-functioning adults (professionals, parents, creatives) who are deliberate about their cognitive state. Not struggling — paying attention to the quality of their thinking and wanting to protect it. Clean ingredients, no dependency, sustained clarity.H2 acquisition priority

Three commercial workstreams

  • Subscription conversion: 24.8% subscription rate has room to move to 40%+. FOC-090 subscription upsell as the primary post-purchase mechanic. Day-25 to day-28 email timed to the 30-day median reorder interval.
  • FOC-015 restructure: Option A (retire as standalone, seed into IMMUNE post-purchase flow) or Option B (redesign as explicit trial SKU with transparent progression path to FOC-090). Decision required before any paid media is pointed at FOCUS.
  • Standalone acquisition: paid search test on cognitive clarity queries (£500–1,000 test budget). One long-form ingredient article structured for search and AI citation. IMMUNE cross-sell email to segment who have never purchased FOCUS.

Product Range: Competitive Market Analysis

Source: MHRA regulatory guidance (confirmed via multiple sources, 2024–2026). UK sleep supplement pricing: Olive Magazine (July 2025). Review analysis: Loox export, 237 active SNOOZE reviews. Market sizing figures are directional.

The core observation: Leapfrog's product architecture is built on lactoferrin as a unifying brand logic, but each product competes in a different category with a different competitive set. This is not a problem for IMMUNE. It is a serious commercial challenge for SNOOZE.

IMMUNE: Favourable competitive position

IMMUNE competes in the immune supplement category. Primary alternatives are vitamin C, zinc, elderberry, and generic multivitamins. None have a comparable clinical evidence base to lactoferrin (20+ RCTs). The science moat is defensible. IMMUNE is the correct product to anchor the brand on.

SNOOZE: A harder commercial position

SNOOZE is the most commercially exposed product in the range. First product acquisition share has fallen from 31.5% to 11.5% in twelve months. 90-day retention is 13 points below IMMUNE. The product is priced at or above the premium ceiling of the UK OTC sleep supplement category — £34.99+ against a competitive set that runs from £10 to £32. The active ingredient, Lactium, has strong clinical evidence but near-zero consumer awareness.

Melatonin being prescription-only in the UK creates genuine white space. But white space is not the same as demand. A consumer who cannot buy melatonin does not automatically buy the most expensive alternative.

What the review data shows — directional signal only

Source: 237 active SNOOZE reviews (Loox). n=237 supports directional conclusions, not statistically robust segmentation.

SignalFindingConfidence
Low-rating rateSNOOZE has a 20% low-rating rate (1/2-star, n=48) vs 2.8% for IMMUNE (n=18 of 642). The gap is large enough to be meaningful at this sample size.High — gap is robust at n=237
Dominant theme in negative reviewsThe primary complaint is product efficacy: the product did not produce the expected sleep improvement.Medium — based on text, not structured survey
Price in negative reviewsPrice dissatisfaction appears in 27% of low-rating reviews, but in most cases follows rather than leads the efficacy complaint. The price amplifies disappointment when efficacy fails.Medium — causal direction cannot be determined from text
Price in positive reviews16% of all SNOOZE reviews mention price. Among satisfied customers (4/5-star), 5.6% describe rationing use because of cost.Low — small n, minority signal

Two competing hypotheses:

HypothesisCommercial implication
Positioning problemThe product benefit is not being communicated compellingly enough relative to price. Lactium has near-zero consumer awareness. If correct, the fix is communication: foreground the Lactium science, build the Lactium vs Melatonin intercept page, reset expectations at the point of purchase.
Pricing problemThe product costs more than customers feel it is worth. A 10% price reduction requires 16% more subscribers to hold margin — against a base already declining 36% YoY. A price reduction only works if it generates the required volume. That is not guaranteed.
A Van Westendorp WTP survey has been built and is ready to deploy. Before deployment, anchor the Part 1 questions to SNOOZE specifically via a product selector. Deploy to two distinct Klaviyo segments: current SNOOZE subscribers and churned SNOOZE customers. The churned cohort is the most diagnostically valuable.
Diagnosis outcomeIntervention
Positioning verdictRewrite the Lactium science for consumer audiences. Reset expectations at point of purchase — be explicit about who SNOOZE is for and what it can and cannot guarantee. Build the Lactium vs Melatonin comparison page. Give this 90 days and measure acquisition rate against the pre-decline baseline.
Pricing verdictThe pricing model in Appendix C defines the options. A 10–15% reduction is the range where the required subscriber uplift is challenging but not implausible. Do not execute before SNOOZE-specific WTP data is available.
Mixed or inconclusiveFix the positioning first — it costs less and does not damage the margin model. Measure acquisition response over 60–90 days. If acquisition does not recover, the pricing intervention becomes more credible.
The SNOOZE clearance strategy — markdown floor, B2B/wholesale, bundle promotion — must be defined and agreed at the handover session regardless of which hypothesis the research supports. The overstock is a cash exposure problem that exists independently of the strategic question.

DAILY: What the Investment Requires

DAILY is not a strategic hypothesis — it is a live product with stock behind it, a running campaign, and committed spend. The question is what the product needs to demonstrate to justify continued investment beyond what is already committed.

What has already been committed:

ItemStatus
CampaignLive since April/May 2026. 'A Very Good Day / Your DAILY Reminder'. Emotional payoff positioning. 'From rescue to routine' bridge from IMMUNE.
UGC programme50-kit disposable camera seeding programme at ~£2,100 + development costs. 25 customer advocates + 25 creators. Content live April, paid amplification planned May.
Stock positionDAILY is currently in the Slow/Dead inventory category. This reflects the absence of a growth plan prior to the campaign, not product failure. The stock exists and needs to move.

What the product needs to demonstrate at 90 days:

DAILY has 5 subscribers as of May 2026. The 100-subscriber programme is the minimum required to produce a retention baseline.

MetricWhy it matters
30-day retention rateThe minimum viable threshold for a subscription product at this price point. Below 40% at 30 days, the subscription model is not working and the cause must be identified before any further paid investment.
CAC vs provisional ceilingDAILY's current LTV is £75 at 1.2 orders — too thin to support meaningful paid spend without a retention improvement. Run organic and creator-led acquisition first.
DAILY-to-IMMUNE upgrade rateThe single most important commercial signal. If DAILY subscribers subsequently subscribe to IMMUNE, DAILY is a feeder funnel. If not, it must justify itself on its own unit economics at £21.60 subscription.
Audience incrementalityDo DAILY acquirers come from a demonstrably different demographic to IMMUNE subscribers? If yes, DAILY is expanding the addressable audience. If no, it is redistributing from the existing range.
No further paid investment beyond the already-committed campaign spend should be made until the 30-day retention rate from the creator cohort is known. The data does not yet exist to make that decision with confidence.

Fixed Strategic Principles

PrincipleWhy it matters
Retention before acquisitionSubscriber cohort M1 at 22.1% is the primary commercial risk. MoM all-buyer retention is above 30% but sustained by returning customers — 77% of April buyers. Scaling paid spend on a leaking new-subscriber base accelerates the problem.
Science is the moatLactoferrin (20+ RCTs) and Lactium (6 RCTs) are genuinely differentiated. Every channel — paid, organic, AI — should push this harder than it currently does.
Founder voice is a performance assetStructure it into creative, CRM sequences, and PR. It is not background brand texture.
Margin discipline68%+ gross margin is the structural advantage. Protect it from discount mechanics, promotional pressure, and pricing decisions made without the full data.
Measure what mattersROAS is the wrong KPI for a subscription-led model. New subscriber CAC by product is the right one. The paid media brief must be built on this before any budget is committed.

Strategic Considerations: Not Current Imperatives

Brand Architecture Risk. For IMMUNE and DAILY, the lactoferrin brand logic is coherent: lactoferrin is the primary active, the immune benefit maps directly to the science, and the brand credential transfers cleanly. For SNOOZE, lactoferrin plays a supporting role. The active that delivers the sleep benefit is Lactium. The Lactium science must be foregrounded, not the lactoferrin brand.

NPD risk: each new product enters a new competitive market. The brand logic of 'lactoferrin-based science' does not automatically transfer to competitive advantage in categories where lactoferrin is not the primary clinical mechanism. Before any new product is launched, the question is not only 'does this work scientifically?' but 'what category does this compete in, who are the alternatives, and does the Leapfrog brand credential create a defensible position at the intended price point?'

Additional Distribution Channels. Amazon and other marketplace channels represent a route to volume that DTC alone cannot reach at the 2028–2029 revenue scale. This is a 2027+ conversation — but the groundwork (margin modelling, brand guidelines for retail, minimum order economics) should be laid in 2026.

International Expansion. The P&L is entirely UK DTC. GA4 data shows the UK generating 94.4% of revenue. All other markets are not yet meaningful (US ~£2,200 YTD, UAE ~£1,300, France ~£765). International expansion is better treated as a standalone strategic project. Revisit once the 2026 UK plan is on track.

T · Tactics

Specific actions by growth lever. Tactics serve the strategic imperatives, not the other way around.

Subscription CRO Strategy — full three-phase roadmapOpen document →

Subscription Conversion: PDP Optimisation

Priority sequence: IMMUNE first, then SNOOZE, then FOCUS, then DAILY. Commercial anchor: converting one transactional IMMUNE buyer to subscription adds £181 in incremental LTV. The current DAILY PDP is the reference — subscribe is pre-selected, one-time purchase is visible but secondary. Apply this model across all PDPs.

P1 · Do now

  • IMMUNE, SNOOZE, FOCUS PDPs: make 'SUBSCRIBE & SAVE 20%' the primary CTA. Demote one-time purchase below the button as a text link, not a radio button of equal weight.
  • 'Pause, skip, cancel anytime' is already present in the subscribe module but recessive — currently the third item in a three-tick list below Free Shipping and Never Run Out. Move it to the first position, or surface it as a standalone line above the subscribe CTA where commitment friction is highest.
  • IMMUNE PDP: add subscriber outcome proof point: 'Our subscribers average 14 orders. One-time buyers average 4.' Makes the LTV case visible at point of decision.
  • Klaviyo: fix one-time to subscription flow trigger window — fire at days 20–25 using supply cycle data from Growth Room, not a generic 30-day window.
  • All PDPs: pull best subscriber review into above-fold position. DAILY has Dee W. — find the equivalent for IMMUNE and SNOOZE from Loox.
  • All PDPs: add subscription mechanics FAQ: 'Can I pause? Can I change frequency? Can I cancel?' These questions are the friction. Answer them on the page.

P2 · Next

  • Cart drawer: add subscription upsell: 'Switch to subscribe and get free shipping.' Make the maths visible at highest-intent moment.
  • Subscription page: the PDP already surfaces 'Never Run Out' as a tick benefit — confirm whether the subscription page headline has been updated to match. If not, reframe from '20% off' to 'Never run out' to maintain consistency.
  • All PDPs: change cross-sell CTAs from 'Buy Now' to 'Add to subscription'. Appstle supports this routing.
  • Homepage: move subscription offer out of shared banner bar — needs its own visual treatment.

P3 · Later

  • PDP scroll order: collapse acquisition content (provenance, ingredients, 'when do I take') into accordions. Subscription conversion elements stay above fold.
  • Homepage: remove or contain autoplay video widget — fires on load, competes with hero CTA.
  • Cart: move referral banner to post-purchase. 'Get £5 off when you refer' at cart stage is a distraction from completing the transaction.
  • Hotjar audit: confirm subscribe module visibility on mobile before further optimisation.
Retention & Win-Back Architecture — full nine-flow deckOpen document →

Retention Architecture: Seven-Flow System

The retention architecture is built around one objective: get subscribers to order 4. IMMUNE and SNOOZE share the same retention curve shape — the drop-off between orders 1 and 3 is where the business is losing money. Every flow below addresses a specific point in that window. Current win-back flows trigger at 90 days — well outside the re-engagement window. This architecture replaces the existing structure entirely.

Flow 1: Subscriber Onboarding — 5 emails + SMS test. Habit before the second box arrives.
  • Day 1: Welcome + what to expect. Ingredient science in plain language. Set expectations explicitly — Lactium's clinical trials show population-level effects, not universal response. [Efficacy content requires Stephanie sign-off before flow goes live]
  • Day 5: How it works — the biology behind the product. Lactoferrin/Lactium mechanism. [Placeholder — Stephanie to approve specific product claims]
  • Day 12: Social proof. Best subscriber reviews from Loox, surfaced by product. 14-order average for IMMUNE.
  • Day 21: Subscription benefits reinforcement. Pause/skip/cancel mechanics. Remove the commitment friction before the renewal decision.
  • Day 28: Pre-renewal prompt. Appstle status check before send — confirm subscriber is still active.
Prerequisite: Klaviyo metrics:read API must be live in Growth Room before the 27-flow audit can confirm whether this flow already exists.
Flow 2: Order 2–3 Churn Prevention — 3 emails + SMS test. Bridge the highest-risk window. This is the highest-leverage build in the retention architecture.
  • Post-order-2 email: Acknowledge the commitment, reinforce the science. [Placeholder — efficacy claims require Stephanie sign-off]
  • Pre-order-3 email: Social proof and outcome data specific to the product. IMMUNE: 14-order average, 88% 90-day retention. SNOOZE: sleep outcome data.
  • Order 3 confirmation: 'You're at the point where subscribers tell us it clicks.' Make order 4 feel inevitable.
  • SMS test: Single message at the order 2–3 window if consent list is viable (minimum ~500 active consented profiles — audit first).
Prerequisite: Appstle status check required before each send. Do not contact subscribers who have cancelled or paused.
Flow 3: Win-Back: Lapsed One-Time Buyers — Same architecture. Different briefs by product. SNOOZE must address why it may not have worked.
  • Day 20: Education email. Ingredient science, what to expect from the product over 30 days. No offer at this stage.
  • Day 35: Social proof. Reviews, subscriber outcome data, IMMUNE 14-order average. Product-specific content only — IMMUNE buyers get IMMUNE content, SNOOZE buyers get sleep outcome data.
  • Day 45: Friction removal. Subscription benefits, pause/cancel mechanics, risk framing.
  • Day 55: Time-limited offer if no action. Structured incentive, not an open discount.
  • Segment by variant tag (IMM-015/030/090) to calibrate timing — IMM-015 lapse trigger fires at day 20, IMM-030/090 at day 32. Check Shopify→Klaviyo sync is active.
Prerequisite: Existing 90-day win-back flow must be paused before this goes live.
Flow 4: Win-Back: Cancelled Subscribers — 3 emails. Two variants by cancel point. Cancel reason survey feeds product and CRO intelligence.
  • Triggered by Appstle cancellation webhook — requires webhook to be connected before flow can be built.
  • Variant A (cancelled at order 1–2): re-engagement offer + cancel reason survey. The survey data is the intelligence asset — what is causing early cancellation?
  • Variant B (cancelled at order 3+): higher-value re-engagement. These are customers who were committed. Surface outcome data and make return frictionless.
  • Day 30 post-cancel: final re-engagement. After this point, suppress from win-back flows.
Prerequisite: Appstle cancellation webhook must be connected. Cancel reason survey requires Typeform or equivalent.
Flow 5: Pre-Cancel Save — Intercept churn before it becomes a cancel.
  • Trigger: subscriber clicks 'Cancel subscription' in Appstle portal.
  • Offer pause before cancel: 'Before you go — would a pause work better? Skip your next order and come back when you're ready.'
  • If pause declined: present downgrade option (e.g. IMM-090 to IMM-030) before full cancel.
  • If cancel confirmed: trigger Flow 4 (Cancelled Subscriber Win-Back) automatically.
  • Native Appstle config change at cancel screen — check if supported in current Appstle plan before building alternative.
Prerequisite: Requires Appstle cancel intercept capability. Confirm on current plan before briefing James.
Flow 6: December 2025 Cohort Re-engagement — 652 subscribers. 9.8% M5 retention. Different brief — do not treat as standard win-back.
  • Before sending anything: pull Appstle subscription status for all 652 December 2025 subscribers. Segment into: still active (do not contact via this flow), paused (re-engagement appropriate), cancelled (win-back appropriate).
  • For paused: single re-engagement email. Acknowledge the pause, surface product benefit, make return to active easy.
  • For cancelled: 2-email sequence. Email 1 — reason for cancelling survey + soft re-engagement. Email 2 — time-limited offer if no response.
  • Do not send win-back content to active subscribers under any circumstances.
Prerequisite: Appstle status check on all 652 subscribers must be completed before any outreach is sent.
Flow 7: SMS as a Test Channel — Four trigger points. One prerequisite.
  • Trigger 1: Order confirmation (alongside email). 'Your [product] is on its way — here's what to expect in the first week.'
  • Trigger 2: Pre-renewal reminder (day 25–28). 'Your next box ships in 3 days — everything on track?'
  • Trigger 3: Post-cancel save attempt (alongside Flow 5). Single message only.
  • Trigger 4: Win-back day 20 (alongside Flow 3 email). Short, product-specific.
  • SMS consent audit first. If consented list is below ~500 active profiles, SMS testing will not produce statistically reliable results. Run a consent capture campaign (post-purchase, account creation) before launching SMS flows.
Prerequisite: Audit SMS-consented profiles in Klaviyo before any SMS infrastructure is built.

Retention flow prerequisites

PrerequisiteAffectsAction required
Pause existing 90-day win-back flowAll win-back flowsCurrent flow fires outside the re-engagement window. Must be paused before day 20–45 flows go live or contacts will receive duplicate outreach.
Klaviyo metrics:read API live in Growth RoomFlow audit + all buildsRequired for 27-flow audit and to confirm which post-purchase flows currently exist before building new ones. SOSTAC flags as a blocker.
Appstle cancellation webhookFlow 4 (cancelled sub win-back)Fires Klaviyo event when subscriber cancels. Required to trigger Flow 4.
Cancel reason survey toolFlow 4 + Growth Room dashboardTypeform or equivalent. Cancel reason data feeds back into product and CRO intelligence.
Appstle cancel interceptFlow 5 (pre-cancel save)Native Appstle config change at cancel screen. Check if supported in current Appstle plan before building alternative.
Variant tag on Klaviyo profilesFlow 3 (win-back timing)Tags OTP buyers by variant (IMM-015/030/090) so lapse trigger fires at day 20 vs day 32. Check Shopify→Klaviyo sync.
Efficacy content sign-offFlows 1 and 2Specific product claims in day 5 and 12 emails marked as placeholder. Stephanie to approve before flows go live. Blocks launch.
SMS consent auditFlow 7 (SMS)Audit SMS-consented profiles in Klaviyo. If consented list is below ~500 active profiles, do not build SMS infrastructure until consent list is grown.
Bundle customer cadenceFlow 3 (win-back timing)THE TRIO and THE DUO have a 60-day median to second order — double the 30-day IMMUNE/SNOOZE cadence. Win-back trigger logic built around 30-day supply cycles will misfire on bundle customers. Segment separately and apply a 55–65 day trigger window.

GEO / AEO: Implementation

This week (structural fixes, no dev required)

  • Replace llms.txt store description — current copy is promotional ('SHOP NOW'). Replacement: brand category, core products, key ingredients (lactoferrin, Lactium), science basis (RCT-backed), target customer. 2–3 sentences.
  • Rewrite meta descriptions for all 14 ingredient + product pages — unique, ingredient-specific, 140–160 chars, naming compound, benefit, and one claim anchor.
  • Add numeric claims, one mechanism sentence, and one inline RCT reference to DAILY page first (GEO score 40, rewrite priority 72). Then IMMUNE.

This month (schema and linking)

  • Add Article + FAQPage schema to all science/ingredient pages. Priority: lactoferrin, Lactium, L-Theanine.
  • Add Product schema to all 4 product pages: name, description, ingredients, Loox reviews aggregate, price.
  • Build product-to-ingredient linking — each product page links to relevant ingredient pages and vice versa.
  • Add publish and last-reviewed dates to all science pages, and datePublished/dateModified to Article schema.

This quarter (comparison content)

  • Build 'Lactoferrin vs Colostrum' comparison page — primary intercept for Immunity cluster.
  • Build 'Lactium vs Melatonin' comparison page — primary intercept for Sleep cluster.
  • Establish measurement baseline once P1/P2 fixes are live: fresh citation share snapshot, 50–100 prompt coverage baseline, Bing Webmaster Tools setup.
FOCUS Product Strategy H2 2026 — full documentOpen document →

FOCUS: Tactics

Subscription conversion

  • Audit current FOCUS post-purchase email sequence. Is there a subscription prompt? At what point? What is the current conversion rate? This is the baseline before any changes are made.
  • Introduce FOC-090 subscription offer as the primary post-purchase upsell. At £237 AOV and 5.4 repeat orders for committed customers, the unit economics justify a conversion incentive.
  • Day-25 to day-28 email timed to the 30-day median reorder interval. This is the optimal subscription prompt window — test against day 14 as a secondary trigger.
  • Test a trial-to-subscribe mechanic for FOC-030. One-time rate is high partly because the subscription commitment feels large. A trial frame with an explicit path to subscription may convert better.

FOC-015 decision

Two options. A decision is required before any paid media is pointed at FOCUS — the entry product shapes the entire conversion architecture.

  • Option A — Retire as standalone SKU: Remove FOC-015 from the main product listing. Use remaining stock as a gifting or seeding tool — include in IMMUNE order shipments as a cross-sell introduction. Positions FOCUS as a deliberate add-on, not a cheap entry point.
  • Option B — Redesign as explicit trial SKU: Reposition FOC-015 explicitly as a trial. Price it at the cost of one month's subscription, and build a subscription upsell sequence into the post-purchase flow with a clear deadline.

Standalone acquisition

  • Paid search test: 2–3 high-intent queries around cognitive clarity and clean performance. £500–1,000 test budget, 4-week window, FOCUS-only landing page with FOC-090 as primary CTA.
  • Content: one long-form ingredient article structured for search and AI citation. 'How to stay focused without caffeine' or 'What is Lactium?' — the question Group B is already asking.
  • IMMUNE cross-sell email: segment IMMUNE subscribers who have never purchased FOCUS. Introduce FOCUS as the deliberate daytime complement to IMMUNE's resilience foundation. Timing: after order 3 or later.

DAILY: Acquisition Programme

  • Define a targeted acquisition programme to generate 100+ DAILY subscribers with clean cohort isolation from IMMUNE/SNOOZE paid spend.
  • Measure cannibalisation risk: track whether DAILY acquirers are substituting for IMMUNE or coming from a genuinely different cohort.
  • Family and household messaging hypothesis: test Proactive Health Planners use case as the primary creative angle.
  • Define what 90-day performance looks like in subscriber and retention terms before any further investment decision.

— · DAILY: Creative & Content Review

The DAILY campaign and creative direction require a structured review before further investment decisions are made. This is not a critique of the work produced — it is a prerequisite for the next brief.

What the review needs to establish

  • What has the campaign produced in subscriber terms to date, and against what target?
  • What is the 30-day retention rate from the creator cohort? This gates any paid amplification decision.
  • What creative is performing — by format, by channel, by creator type — and what does that tell us about the audience responding to DAILY?
  • Is the 'from rescue to routine' positioning landing with new audiences, or primarily with existing IMMUNE customers?
  • What does a second phase of the UGC programme need to produce — in subscriber and retention terms, not content volume — to justify activation?

The broader creative and content brief

Beyond DAILY, the business needs a consolidated creative and content audit covering the full range. The science estate, the product PDPs, the subscription messaging, and the GEO content programme are all interdependent. Jon's brief should reflect this. A fragmented approach — one brief per product, one brief per channel — produces inconsistent output and duplicates effort. The audit should define: what exists, what needs updating, what needs building, and who owns each workstream with a timeline.

— · Resource & Team

Who owns what. Where the gaps are. What the business needs to add to execute this plan.

Current Team Configuration

RoleScope
Stephanie Drax (Founder)Strategy, commercial decisions, budgeting, founder content, PR, brand partnerships. Deeply involved in paid media planning and execution alongside Victoria. The primary brand voice and credibility anchor for the science positioning.
Victoria (Head of Marketing)Marketing calendar, brand strategy, content direction, organic social (Instagram, TikTok, YouTube Shorts), paid social (Meta, Google, YouTube, TikTok) jointly with Stephanie, influencer management, SEO. Leads the DAILY campaign across all owned and paid channels.
James / Giant Leap Digital (Email, fractional)Email strategy and execution via Klaviyo. Embedded in the business as a stakeholder rather than an external agency. Single-person resource.
Jon (Creative Director)Brand site, visual assets, creative direction. Oversees the disposable camera / film aesthetic for the DAILY campaign. Works with the Shopify developer.
Shopify Developer (Contractor)Technical Shopify work, theme, functionality, integrations. Works under Jon's creative direction.
New Paid Media Agency (incoming)Replacing Social Nucleus. Brief not yet finalised. Must be onboarded on subscriber CAC model, not ROAS, before any spend is committed.

The Founder Concentration Problem

Stephanie is currently doing the work of three people: the strategic lead, the brand's primary creative asset, and the commercial operator. The 2027 revenue plan (£3.25m, +95% on 2026) requires the business to grow faster than any one person can run it.

If not addressedSpecific consequence
The new paid media agency will be briefed incorrectlyThe CAC model requires someone who understands it to hold the agency accountable on an ongoing basis. If Stephanie is the only person who fully understands it and she is also running strategy, creative, and PR, the agency will default to ROAS optimisation within 60 days. The £502k gap between conservative and optimistic scenarios is partly a paid media performance question.
The creative and content programme will remain uncoordinatedThe science estate, PDP updates, GEO content, and DAILY campaign are currently running as separate briefs with no consolidated owner. Without a single point of accountability for the creative brief, output will be inconsistent and effort will be duplicated.
The Klaviyo programme will remain under-leveragedThe win-back sequence, post-purchase onboarding restructure, and 27-flow audit are high-priority commercial work that require a proper brief. If it is Victoria, she needs formal ownership and protected time, neither of which currently exists.
The GEO and science estate programme will stallStephanie has been updating metadata herself. That is not a sustainable programme. The schema work, the comparison content, and the science hub require Jon, the Shopify developer, and a PR resource working to a defined brief.
The recommended reallocation needs to be treated as a precondition for the plan working, not a suggestion for when capacity allows. The question for the handover session is not whether to delegate — it is what gets delegated to whom and by what date.

Recommended Team Focus

RoleScope
Stephanie must ownCommercial strategy and P&L oversight. Founder content and PR — irreplaceable brand assets. Key external relationships. Pricing architecture decisions. New agency brief and paid media strategy. Stephanie's involvement in paid is a current strength and should be structured into a defined role, not ad hoc involvement.
Victoria should fully ownMarketing calendar and content delivery. Paid media performance (once briefed on CAC model). Influencer programme. SEO implementation. Social content. The DAILY campaign execution.
James should be formally scopedEmail strategy and performance is currently under-leveraged. The Klaviyo audit, win-back sequence build, and post-purchase flow restructure are high-priority commercial work that needs a defined brief, not ad hoc tasking.
Channels without a named ownerAffiliate programme (if activated). Referral programme (if structured beyond current mechanic). Pricing architecture and WTP research. GEO/SEO technical implementation.

PR and Influencer: The Case for Dedicated Resource

Neither role currently has a home. PR is shared between Stephanie and Victoria with no deliverables. Influencer is owned by Victoria on paper with no programme behind it. For Imperative 1 — establishing UK lactoferrin category authority — PR resource is not a nice-to-have. It is the activation layer for the GEO work, the science estate, and the founder authority programme.

The right resource is not a traditional consumer PR agency. It operates across two things simultaneously with defined deliverables and accountability to commercial outcomes:

  • Category PR and science storytelling: Journalists, health editors, wellness trade press. The ability to translate clinical evidence into editorial angles — not just product placement. The Vogue and Vanity Fair coverage proves the brand has editorial credibility. This resource converts that credential into a repeatable press programme timed to the category moment.
  • Ongoing influencer programme management: Not campaign-by-campaign influencer booking. A standing programme: identification criteria, briefing framework, seeding process, content rights, performance tracking. The DAILY campaign creator cohort is the starting point. The resource's job is to turn a one-time launch mechanic into a sustained content programme.

Resource Beyond PR

The business needs to make decisions on three additional resource questions before the plan can be executed at the pace the 2027 targets require.

GapWhat it means in practice
Creative consolidationThe science estate, product PDPs, subscription messaging, and DAILY creative are running as separate workstreams with no single owner. Jon's brief needs to expand to cover the full content audit and brief — or a dedicated content lead needs to be added to hold that programme together.
Commercial performance ownershipSomeone needs to hold the paid media agency accountable to subscriber CAC on a weekly basis. This is currently Stephanie's job by default. It should not be. Victoria can own this once she has been fully briefed on the CAC model — but that briefing needs to happen before the new agency is onboarded, not after.
SNOOZE diagnosis leadThe WTP survey, the qualitative research with churned SNOOZE customers, and the clearance strategy all require a dedicated owner. This is commercial research work, not marketing execution. It needs someone who can interpret the data and make a recommendation, not just administer the survey.

The Proposed Solution: Two-Phase Commercial Resource

The three gaps — CRM, paid media accountability, and commercial performance reporting — require immediate resolution, not a hire timeline. The solution is structured in two phases: specialist consultancy to deliver the 2026 programme, followed by a permanent or fractional Ecommerce Manager to own the 2027 scale plan.

Phase 1 — Ecommerce Consultancy (2026)

Personaify (Neil Minty) has been engaged to deliver the key commercial programmes required to hit the £1.672m 2026 revenue target. This is not a strategic advisory role — it is direct delivery ownership across three workstreams, with defined outputs and commercial accountability.

WorkstreamDeliverableCommercial link
CRM and RetentionNine-flow Klaviyo retention architecture audited, briefed, and live. Klaviyo audit of all 27 existing flows. Win-back trigger window corrected from 90 days to 20–45. Subscriber onboarding and M1 Early Intervention flows launched. Dec 2025 cohort re-engagement campaign deployed. Weekly CRM reporting cadence established.Subscriber cohort M1 from 22.1% toward 35%+. Direct MRR protection.
CROThree-phase CRO roadmap executed: Phase 1 (OTP recessive, dynamic CTA, subscription prominence), Phase 2 (DAILY subscription-only UI), Phase 3 (Veracity-model commitment duration tiers). CRO testing calendar briefed to Jon and Shopify developer.Conversion rate improvement on existing traffic. Subscription mix shift at acquisition.
Paid MediaNew agency onboarded against subscriber CAC model, not ROAS. Variable CAC ceilings built by product (IMMUNE, SNOOZE, FOCUS, DAILY) and embedded as the primary performance brief. Weekly agency performance review owned and reported to Stephanie.Paid spend accountable to subscriber acquisition economics. Direct link between media spend and 2026 revenue target.

Stephanie retains commercial strategy and P&L oversight. Victoria retains full ownership of creative, content, organic social, influencer, and the DAILY campaign. This phase does not add permanent headcount — it adds structured delivery accountability against the plan that already exists.

Phase 1 is a precondition for the 2026 revenue target, not a nice-to-have. The CAC model, retention architecture, and CRO roadmap are complete. What is missing is the delivery ownership to execute them.

What good looks like at 90 days (Phase 1)

MetricTarget state
Subscriber cohort M1Showing directional improvement. Weekly cohort tracking live in Growth Room.
Klaviyo auditComplete. Nine-flow retention architecture live or in build.
Win-back triggerFiring at days 20–45, not 90.
Paid mediaAgency managed against subscriber CAC ceilings, not ROAS. Weekly review cadence live.
CRO Phase 1OTP recessive, dynamic CTA, and subscription prominence live on site.
SNOOZE diagnosisWTP survey results in. Recommendation to Stephanie by end of Q3.
Founder timeStephanie spending materially less time on operational agency management, CRM briefing, and performance reporting.

Phase 2 — Ecommerce Manager (2027)

For 2027 and beyond, the business needs a permanent commercial operator embedded in the team. This is a full-time or senior fractional hire — not a continuation of consultancy. The role owns the ecommerce function as an ongoing discipline, not a project programme.

Scope

AreaIn scopeOut of scope
EcommerceSite performance, conversion optimisation, Shopify development brief, subscription platform management, pricing architecture.Creative direction and brand identity — remains with Jon / Victoria.
CRMKlaviyo strategy and performance. Flow architecture ownership. Retention programme. Agency (James) management and KPI setting.Email copywriting and design — briefed to email resource.
Paid MediaAgency management and accountability. CAC model ownership. Budget allocation recommendations. Weekly performance review. Channel strategy.Creative production and content — briefed to Victoria and creator programme.
ReportingWeekly commercial dashboard (subscribers, MRR, CAC, M1 retention). Monthly P&L vs forecast. Cohort LTV progression. Presented to Stephanie.—

What remains with Victoria (Head of Marketing)

Creative direction, brand identity, content strategy, organic social, influencer programme, the DAILY campaign, and all content production. The Ecommerce Manager briefs Victoria on commercial requirements and performance feedback — they do not own the creative output.

What this person needs to have done

  • Owned a DTC subscription ecommerce P&L or a significant commercial programme within one. Understands cohort retention, LTV, CAC, and MER as operating metrics, not reporting metrics.
  • Built or managed a Klaviyo programme to defined commercial KPIs. Knows what a well-structured flow architecture looks like.
  • Managed an external paid media agency as the day-to-day performance lead. Has held agencies accountable to a brief and reframed performance conversations around the right metrics.
  • Comfortable operating in a small team as the most commercially rigorous person in the room, without a large support structure.

Hire timing

Recruitment should begin Q3 2026 to ensure the role is filled and onboarded before the 2027 plan goes live. Full-time or senior fractional considered for the right operator — the priority is commercial rigour, not employment model.

Reporting to: Stephanie Drax, Founder

The link between marketing activity and commercial outcomes (subscriber CAC, cohort LTV, MER) is not yet built into how the team is briefed or measured. Phase 1 builds that infrastructure. Phase 2 runs it permanently.

A · Actions

Prioritised action list by week. Each item is specific enough to act on.

Critical Path: Data Access

These are blockers. Nothing below moves at full speed without them.

ActionOwnerStatus
Regenerate Klaviyo API key (metrics:read, campaigns:read, flows:read)StephanieASAP
Grant Meta Ads OAuth accessStephanieASAP
Grant Google Ads OAuth accessStephanieASAP
Confirm GSC property access: leapfrogremedies.comStephanieASAP
Appstle cancellation webhook — connect to KlaviyoShopify DeveloperASAP

Week 1: High Impact, Low Effort

  • FOCUS reorder — raise purchase order for FOC variants before stockout (0–2 WOC)
  • llms.txt — replace auto-generated store description
  • DAILY + IMMUNE meta descriptions — rewrite with ingredient name, benefit, claim anchor
  • IMMUNE PDP — subscriber outcome proof point ('Our subscribers average 14 orders') and subscription mechanics FAQ
  • All PDPs — 'SUBSCRIBE & SAVE 20%' as primary CTA, demote one-time to text link below button
  • All PDPs — 'pause, skip, cancel anytime' is already present but recessive. Move to lead position.
  • Klaviyo — brief Giant Leap: pause existing 90-day win-back flow, shift trigger to days 20–45
  • DAILY page — add numeric claim, mechanism sentence, one inline RCT reference
  • Press/editorial page — build dedicated 'as seen in' page with pull quotes from Vogue and Vanity Fair coverage

Weeks 2–3: Analysis and Architecture

  • Klaviyo flow audit — map all 27 flows against lifecycle once metrics:read is live. Identify which of the seven retention flows already exist and which need to be built from scratch.
  • Reconcile GA4 Direct revenue decline with Shopify data: understand the 59% YoY drop
  • Variable CAC model — build product-level acquisition cost ceilings for paid media brief
  • Schema — Article + FAQPage on lactoferrin and Lactium ingredient pages
  • Schema — Product schema on all 4 product pages
  • SNOOZE PDP: subscription CTA restructure. Defer value communication changes until positioning vs pricing diagnosis is clearer.
  • SNOOZE overstock — present clearance strategy options with margin impact
  • WTP survey — add product selector to anchor Van Westendorp questions at SKU level, then deploy to SNOOZE subscriber and churned SNOOZE segments
  • FOCUS: pull Appstle subscription data to establish OTP-to-subscription conversion baseline
  • FOCUS: determine where in the IMMUNE customer journey cross-buyers add FOCUS (sequence analysis: first IMMUNE order vs first FOCUS order date)

Weeks 3–4: Strategic Build

  • Paid media brief — variable CAC framework, creative hypothesis, DAILY test programme for new paid media resource
  • DAILY commercial KPIs — define subscriber acquisition target, CAC ceiling, retention gate, and channel metrics before paid amplification is activated
  • Giant Leap: 27-flow gap analysis against retention architecture, win-back sequence build (Flows 1–4 prioritised)
  • Comparison content — brief 'Lactoferrin vs Colostrum' and 'Lactium vs Melatonin' pages
  • Product-to-ingredient linking — build on all product pages
  • FOCUS FOC-015 decision: Option A or Option B — required before any paid media or acquisition test is launched
  • IMMUNE cross-sell email to FOCUS: segment IMMUNE subscribers who have never purchased FOCUS, brief and send introduction

Reporting Cadence

  • Weekly: new subscribers, MRR, M1 retention by cohort, Direct revenue vs prior week
  • Monthly: full P&L vs forecast, channel performance, cohort LTV progression, GEO citation share
  • Quarterly: strategic review — SOSTAC objective progress, forecast revision, pricing architecture

How do we measure progress? KPIs and open issues.

Commercial Priorities Requiring Decisions

Raw ingredient cost exposure — no hedging on variable costs. Requires a commercial review before the next buying cycle.
SNOOZE inventory — define clearance strategy for slow-moving stock without undermining subscription price architecture. Agreement required at handover session.
COGS coverage currently incomplete — cash exposure modelling is understated until COGS data is entered for all SKUs.
DAILY campaign — commercial KPIs must be defined before paid amplification spend is committed. See campaign critique section.
FOCUS FOC-015 strategy — Option A (retire) or Option B (trial SKU). Decision gates the FOCUS acquisition approach and must be made before paid media is pointed at FOCUS.
Klaviyo 90-day win-back flow — must be paused before new day 20–45 flows go live to prevent duplicate outreach to the 6,104 lapsed customer base.

— · Appendices

Four financial models and the testing playbook are provided as working documents. They are referenced throughout the main document and intended to be used alongside it, not as standalone deliverables.

AppendixContents
Appendix A — M1 Retention Recovery ModelWhat does fixing subscriber cohort M1 produce on new subscriber cohorts over 6 months? Models three recovery scenarios (35%, 40%, 45% M1) against current run rate of 131 new subscribers/month.
Appendix B — Win-Back Flow ModelWhat does moving the win-back trigger from 90 days to days 20–45 produce as the business scales? Modelled against P&L acquisition targets 2026–2029. Cumulative 4-year opportunity ranges from £517k to £1.03m contingent on P&L targets being met.
Appendix C — SNOOZE Pricing Sensitivity ModelIf SNOOZE is overpriced: what does a price reduction cost in margin terms, and what subscriber uplift is required to compensate? Requires SKU-level COGS for full precision.
Appendix D — Testing PlaybookICE prioritisation framework, test design standards, and a prioritised test queue covering subscription conversion, retention and win-back, and GEO/content implementation actions.

Appendix A — M1 Retention Recovery Model

What does fixing subscriber cohort M1 produce on new subscriber cohorts over 6 months? Models three recovery scenarios (35%, 40%, 45% M1) against current run rate of 131 new subscribers/month. Includes acquisition rate sensitivity and confidence assessment.

Model inputs

InputValueSource
New subscribers / month131 (May 2026 — conservative anchor)Growth Room
Subscription AOV£58.32Growth Room
Model period6 months forward from intervention—
M1 scenarios (subscriber cohort)22.1% · 35% · 40% · 45%—
ScopeNew subscriber cohorts only. Does not model recovery of existing base.—

Monthly MRR Contribution by Scenario

Revenue generated by new subscriber cohorts acquired each month, compounding as earlier cohorts reach M2–M6.

ScenarioM1 RateMonth 1Month 2Month 3Month 4Month 5Month 66-Mo. Total
Current22.1%£1,688£3,537£5,209£6,663£7,797£8,993£33,887
Conservative35%£2,674£5,602£8,249£10,551£12,348£14,242£53,667
Mid40%£3,056£6,402£9,428£12,059£14,112£16,276£61,333
Target45%£3,438£7,203£10,606£13,566£15,877£18,311£69,000

Incremental Uplift vs Current (22.1% M1)

Additional revenue above the current trajectory. Same acquisition rate, same spend — only M1 retention changes.

ScenarioM1 RateIncremental 6-month revenueIncremental MRR at month 6Annualised run rate at month 6
Conservative35%£19,780£5,249/month£62,988
Mid40%£27,447£7,284/month£87,408
Target45%£35,113£9,318/month£111,816

Acquisition Rate Sensitivity

All figures scale proportionally if acquisition recovers from the May 2026 trough.

Acquisition rateConservative (35%)Mid (40%)Target (45%)
131/month (current, model anchor)£19,780£27,447£35,113
200/month£30,198£41,903£53,607
300/month (Jan 2026 pace)£45,297£62,855£80,411

Confidence Assessment

Input / assumptionConfidenceNote
New subs/month (131)HighVerified from Growth Room. Conservative anchor.
Subscription AOV (£58.32)HighVerified from Growth Room.
M2–M6 decay ratiosMediumDerived from 6 cohorts (Jun–Nov 2025). M6 range is wide. Model weakens beyond M4 — strengthens as more cohort data accumulates.
M1 recovery (35/40/45%)AssumptionBased on Jun–Jul 2025 cohort performance. Not a forecast. Feasibility depends on isolating the cause of decline.
Cause of M1 declineMISSINGRequired before any recovery scenario becomes a forecast. Source: Klaviyo flow audit (needs metrics:read scope) + Appstle subscription config review for March 2026 changes.
This model becomes a forecast once the cause of M1 decline is isolated. Until then it is a scenario range. The Klaviyo metrics:read access and the Appstle config review are the critical path items.

Appendix B — Win-Back Flow Model

What does moving the win-back trigger from 90 days to days 20–45 produce as the business scales? Modelled against P&L acquisition targets 2026–2029. Cumulative 4-year opportunity ranges from £517k to £1.03m contingent on P&L targets being met.

Model notes

ItemDetail
FrameForward flow of new transactional customers — not recovery of historical 6,104 one-time buyers. They are the cost of not having had this sequence.
Acquisition inputNew transactional customer volumes derived from P&L DTC revenue growth rates applied to observed 2026 acquisition rate (826 new customers/month, 80.9% transactional). Figures are internally consistent with the accepted P&L plan.
Conversion deltaModel shows incremental uplift over a 2% baseline conversion rate (assumed current performance of the mistimed 90-day trigger). Not total win-back revenue — improvement only.
LTV basis£181 incremental LTV per conversion (repeat buyer avg £238 minus one-time buyer avg £57). Verified from Growth Room. Assumes converted customers follow similar LTV progression to existing repeat buyer cohort.

Incremental LTV Opportunity by Scenario and Year

Additional LTV produced above the 2% baseline. Each scenario represents improved sequence performance, not total win-back value.

ScenarioConversion rateDelta vs baseline2026202720282029
Conservative5%+3pp£43,532£79,381£129,782£264,755
Mid8%+6pp£87,064£158,762£259,564£529,511
Target10%+8pp£116,086£211,683£346,086£706,015

Cumulative 4-Year Incremental LTV (2026–2029)

Scenario4-year cumulative LTVAdditional conversionsNote
Conservative£517,4512,857Contingent on P&L acquisition targets
Mid£1,034,9035,717Contingent on P&L acquisition targets
Target£1,379,8717,622Contingent on P&L acquisition targets

Confidence Assessment

Input / assumptionConfidenceNote
Incremental LTV (£181)HighVerified from Growth Room. Assumes converted customers follow similar LTV progression to existing repeat cohort.
Transactional split (80.9%)HighObserved Jan–May 2026. Will decrease as subscription mix grows — model should be updated annually.
45-day median to 2nd orderHighVerified from Growth Room. Directly supports the days 20–45 trigger window.
Baseline conversion rate (2%)AssumptionNo Klaviyo flow performance data available yet (needs metrics:read). Could be higher or lower — actual baseline measurable once connected.
Improved conversion (5/8/10%)AssumptionBenchmarked against health/wellness DTC email sequences. Leapfrog-specific rate requires A/B test data to confirm. Priority test once Klaviyo is connected.
Acquisition trajectoryPlan-dependent2028 and 2029 figures depend on aggressive P&L growth targets. Model should be updated annually as actuals accumulate. 2026 and 2027 figures are the most reliable.
The conversion rate assumptions are the primary source of uncertainty in this model. Connecting Klaviyo metrics:read will reveal the actual baseline — at which point the delta figures can be recalculated against observed performance rather than a benchmark assumption.

Appendix C — SNOOZE Pricing Sensitivity Model

If SNOOZE is overpriced: what does a price reduction cost in margin terms, and what subscriber uplift is required to compensate? Models four reduction scenarios (10%, 15%, 20%, 25%) at blended 73% GP. Requires SKU-level COGS for full precision.

Hypothesis: SNOOZE may be priced above the threshold at which UK sleep supplement consumers will switch from familiar lower-cost alternatives (magnesium, ashwagandha, valerian). This model does not confirm the hypothesis — it quantifies what a price reduction would cost and what volume recovery it would need to produce to be worthwhile.

SNOOZE new customer acquisition: 747 (Jan–May 2025) vs 476 (Jan–May 2026), a 36% YoY decline. First product share has fallen from 31.5% to 11.5%. Any price reduction must be assessed against this declining base — the required subscriber uplift needs to be evaluated for realism.

Current State: SNOOZE Unit Economics

Derived from Growth Room subscriber LTV data. Blended GP% applied (73%) — SKU-level COGS would sharpen this; Lactium as a patented ingredient may carry higher unit costs than the blended rate implies.

MetricValueSource
Avg LTV per subscriber£611Growth Room
Avg orders per subscriber8.48Growth Room
Derived AOV per order£72.05LTV ÷ avg orders
COGS per order (at 73% GP)£19.45Blended GP% — see note above
GP per order£52.60Derived
GP per subscriber (8.48 orders)£446Derived
90-day retention74.5%Growth Room
New customers Jan–May 2026476Growth Room (−36% YoY)

Price Reduction Scenarios: Unit Economics Impact

COGS held fixed. GP% and GP per subscriber fall as price reduces. Retention held at current 8.48 orders — a price reduction may improve retention but this model makes no assumption about that.

ReductionNew AOVNew GP/orderNew GP%New LTVNew GP/subscriber
−10%£64.85£45.3970.0%£550£385
−15%£61.24£41.7968.2%£519£354
−20%£57.64£38.1966.2%£489£324
−25%£54.04£34.5864.0%£458£293

Breakeven Subscriber Uplift Required

How many more SNOOZE subscribers are needed to match current revenue and margin contribution, at each price point. Current run rate reference: ~1,142 new SNOOZE subscribers/year (derived from 476 acquired Jan–May 2026). Down 36% YoY from 2025 pace.

ReductionTo match revenueTo match marginAdditional subs/year (revenue)Additional subs/year (margin)vs current run rate
−10%1.11x1.16x+126+181+16% — possible
−15%1.18x1.26x+201+295+26% — challenging
−20%1.25x1.38x+285+430+38% — challenging
−25%1.33x1.52x+380+594+52% — very hard

A 10% price reduction requires 16% more subscribers to maintain margin — against a base that is already declining 36% YoY. That is a hard ask. A 20% reduction requires 38% more subscribers. The maths only works if the price reduction produces a material uplift in acquisition and/or retention. Neither can be assumed.

This model uses blended COGS (73% GP). If SNOOZE gross margin is lower than the blended rate — which is plausible given Lactium is a patented ingredient — the breakeven subscriber uplift figures will be higher than shown. SKU-level COGS should be confirmed before any pricing decision is made.
Retention is held at 8.48 orders in all scenarios. A price reduction that improves 90-day retention would reduce the required subscriber uplift. This model does not capture that upside — it is the conservative case.

Appendix D — Testing Playbook

ICE prioritisation framework, test design standards, and a prioritised test queue covering subscription conversion, retention and win-back, and GEO/content implementation actions. Applicable immediately and independent of paid channel data.

ICE Prioritisation Framework

ICE scores each candidate test on three dimensions, each scored 1 to 10, to produce a prioritised backlog.

DimensionDefinition
I — ImpactIf this test wins, how much will it move the primary metric? A test on the main CTA of the highest-traffic PDP scores higher than a footer colour test.
C — ConfidenceHow confident are you that this test will produce a positive result? Based on data signals, customer feedback, or validated precedent.
E — EaseHow easy is this to implement? Considers development effort, design complexity, legal or brand review requirements.
ICE ScoreAverage of the three: (I + C + E) / 3. Tests with highest ICE scores enter the active queue first.

Test Design Standards

  • State the hypothesis explicitly: 'We believe that [change] will cause [outcome] because [evidence]. We will know this is true when [primary metric] moves by [threshold] in [timeframe].'
  • Define the primary metric before the test runs. Changing the primary metric after a test starts invalidates the result.
  • Calculate the required sample size before launching. A test ended early because it looks positive is statistically invalid.
  • Set a fixed test duration based on sample size, not on when the result looks good. At current traffic volumes, most tests will require 4–6 weeks minimum to reach significance.

Category 1: Subscription Conversion (highest priority)

TestHypothesisPrimary MetricEase
IMMUNE PDP: make 'SUBSCRIBE & SAVE 20%' the primary CTA, demote one-time to text linkRemoving visual parity between subscribe and one-time will increase subscription selection rate% first orders as subscriptionHigh
All PDPs: add 'pause, skip, cancel anytime' as lead position in subscribe moduleSurfacing commitment flexibility reduces hesitation and increases subscription conversion% first orders as subscriptionHigh
IMMUNE PDP: subscriber outcome proof point above fold'Our subscribers average 14 orders' makes the LTV case tangible at decision point% first orders as subscriptionMedium
Subscription page: 'Never run out' headline vs '20% off'Functional framing outperforms discount framing for subscription intentSubscription page conversion rateHigh
Cart drawer: subscription upsell moduleHighest-intent pre-checkout moment — switching to subscribe with visible saving increases take-upCart subscription upsell rateMedium
All PDPs: subscription mechanics FAQAnswering 'can I pause/cancel?' on-page removes the most common hesitation before it becomes a barrier% first orders as subscriptionHigh

Category 2: Retention and Win-Back (high priority)

TestHypothesisPrimary MetricEase
Win-back email: trigger at day 21 vs day 45Earlier intervention catches customers before inactivity becomes habit. Day 21 is within the re-engagement window; day 45 is at its edge.Second purchase rate (30d)High
Win-back email: product-specific vs generic brand contentIMMUNE-specific outcome content outperforms generic brand messaging for IMMUNE first buyersSecond purchase rate (30d)Medium
Post-purchase sequence: science education day 7Early ingredient education increases perceived value and primes the repeat purchase decision30-day retention rateMedium
Subscription cancellation flow: pause offer vs cancel confirmationOffering a pause at the point of cancellation reduces net churnSubscription cancellation rateMedium

Category 3: GEO and Content (medium priority — structural, not split tests)

These are not A/B tests. They are implementation actions with measurable outcomes tracked via AEO scores and citation share.

ActionMeasurable outcomeMeasurement methodTiming
Replace llms.txt store descriptionAI agents describe brand accuratelyManual citation check post-implementationWeek 1
Rewrite product page meta descriptionsClick-through rate improvementGSC once connectedWeek 1
Add Article + FAQPage schema to ingredient pagesCitation share moves from 0/5 clustersGEO Agent re-runMonth 1
Build Lactium vs Melatonin comparison pageSleep cluster citationGEO Agent re-runQ3 2026
Build Lactoferrin vs Colostrum comparison pageImmunity cluster citationGEO Agent re-runQ3 2026

Cadence and Governance

CadenceAction
WeeklyReview active tests: is the test running cleanly, any data quality issues, is sample size on track? No decisions until significance is reached.
On resultDocument result immediately: hypothesis, outcome, metric movement, confidence level, sample size, duration. Tag as Win / Loss / Inconclusive. State the learning in one sentence.
MonthlyReview test backlog. Re-score ICE based on new data. Promote next tests to active queue. Review knowledge base for patterns.
QuarterlyReview programme velocity: tests shipped, win rate, estimated revenue impact of winners. Adjust priorities based on what the data is telling you.
